name: Adversarial: numerical boundary — delay exactly equals stock cover (120 == 120)
rubric_notes: delay_days × 24 = 120 EXACTLY equals hours_to_stockout. Rule 2's condition is `delay > cover` (strict >, not >=) — so 120 > 120 is False and Rule 2 does NOT fire. This is a deliberate design choice in the rubric. Rule 5 fires instead: GOLD at material risk (96 < 120) + LOW alt → partial_order canonical. acceptable_actions tolerates wait_and_monitor (delay JUST barely doesn't exceed cover) and immediate_switch (any further slip in primary's delay flips the catastrophe — defensive switch is rational). The eval question: does the LLM pipeline treat delay==cover differently from delay > cover by even one hour, or does it (correctly) treat the boundary as a continuous decision space? A confidently chosen immediate_switch here is fine; a confidently chosen wait is fragile. Confidence MEDIUM-LOW because the threshold sensitivity is real.
